title: Point Reyes Light, April 16, 1959 (Page 2)
author: Point Reyes Light
card_summary: A single digitized page (page 2) from the Point Reyes Light newspaper, published in Point Reyes Station, California on April 16, 1959. Philip K. Dick lived in the Point Reyes area during the late 1950s and early 1960s, making local newspaper coverage from this period potentially relevant to his biography.
page_summary: This document is a digitized scan of page 2 from the Point Reyes Light, a weekly community newspaper published in Point Reyes Station, California, dated April 16, 1959. The image was sourced from Newspapers.com (image ID 1103966557) and downloaded on April 5, 2025. The text layer contains only the Newspapers.com metadata and copyright notice; the substantive content of the newspaper page is embedded in the scanned image and is not machine-readable from the extracted text. This document is of potential biographical significance because Philip K. Dick lived in the Point Reyes Station area during the late 1950s and early 1960s. Dick moved to the area with his second wife, Kleo Apostolides, settling on Lorraine Avenue in Point Reyes Station around 1958-1959. He subsequently lived there with his third wife, Anne Dick (nee Rubenstein), through approximately 1962. During this period, Dick wrote several of his most important works, including The Man in the High Castle (1962), which won the Hugo Award for Best Novel. The Point Reyes Light, as the local newspaper of record for this small Marin County community, may contain references to Dick or his associates, advertisements or notices relevant to the local cultural milieu, or contextual information about the rural Northern California environment that shaped his fiction during one of his most productive periods. As a primary source from Dick's immediate community during a pivotal creative phase, this newspaper page contributes to the broader archival record of his geographic and social context, even if its specific relevance to Dick can only be determined through examination of the scanned image itself.
